ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese beef pho; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002700) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Soup, noodle-based) is the closest parent category; no specific pho code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for pho beef noodle soup or Vietnamese noodle soup at time of processing.

allergens: gluten (possible cross-contamination from soy sauce or hoisin sauce condiments)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of pho beef noodle soup weighs approximately 500 g, comprising broth (~300 g), rice noodles (~100 g), beef slices (~70 g), and garnishes (~30 g).

nutrient_source.title: Pho, beef noodle soup — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry for Vietnamese pho exists. Values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef noodle soup (NDB 06016) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese-style beef noodle soup. Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of broth, rice noodles, and beef in typical proportions (60% broth, 20% noodles, 14% beef, 6% garnish). Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and beef broth composite estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese pho beef noodle soup. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (GI ~40–53, mean ~40 for fresh/boiled rice noodles) and the diluting effect of the high-water, low-carbohydrate broth. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (40 × 30 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 12.0. Rice noodle GI reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283; updated 2021 via University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from bone broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl of pho beef noodle soup provides approximately 1900 mg sodium, representing 83% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit condiment additions such as hoisin and soy sauce, and consume this dish infrequently.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from rice noodles with low-to-moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pho beef noodle soup has a glycemic index of approximately 40 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 12 per standard bowl; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in moderate portions, noting that the high protein and broth volume help moderate postprandial glucose response, but portion size of rice noodles should be controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef and bone broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1900 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~2.5 g per bowl) from beef and bone marrow are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency, choose leaner beef cuts, and avoid adding high-sodium condiments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pho Beef Noodle Soup (Phở Bò) is a Vietnamese noodle soup dish consisting of a rich, aromatic beef bone broth served over flat rice noodles (bánh phở) with thinly sliced beef, fresh herbs, bean sprouts, lime, and chilli. The broth is the defining element, traditionally simmered for 8–12 hours with beef bones, charred ginger, charred onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and fish sauce, producing a deeply savoury, slightly sweet, and spiced flavour profile. The rice noodles are soft and silky, and the beef is typically served rare, cooking in the hot broth at the table. Pho originated in northern Vietnam in the early 20th century and has become one of Vietnam's most iconic national dishes, widely served across Southeast Asia and globally. In Singapore, pho is commonly served in Vietnamese restaurants and food courts. Nutritionally, pho is a moderate-energy dish (~260 kcal per 500 g bowl) with a reasonable protein content (~21 g per bowl) from beef, a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~40) from rice noodles, and a high sodium content (~1900 mg per bowl) from the broth and fish sauce. It is dairy-free and gluten-free in its traditional form, though condiments such as hoisin sauce and soy sauce may introduce gluten. The dish is clinically relevant for sodium management in hypertension and cardiovascular disease, and requires portion control of rice noodles for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of pho?
How much sodium is in pho?
What are the ingredients in pho?
What is the glycemic index of pho?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Vietnamese pho beef noodle soup as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef noodle soup data and FAO/INFOODS SEA compositional references, with proportional weighting applied for typical pho composition. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle values. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy beef noodle soup reference or FAO/INFOODS SEA data for this dish type. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source references used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source references used. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is reported as 0.0; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for beef noodle soup. The cuisine field reflects the dish's Vietnamese origin; the food is served in Singapore by Co Chung Restaurant. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant's halal certification status is not confirmed from approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct pho GI measurement was found in approved sources. Sodium content is notably high due to the bone broth base and fish sauce; this is consistent with published data for Vietnamese pho.
